title: 100 Baggers
subtitle: Stocks That Return 100-to-1 and How to Find Them
author: Christopher W. Mayer
description: An empirical study of stocks that multiplied 100x between 1962 and 2014, revealing the common traits — small size, high returns on capital, owner-operators, and extreme patience — that separate life-changing investments from the rest.
keyTakeaways: Look for small companies (under $300M market cap) with a long runway for growth.
Prioritize owner-operators — management with 10%+ insider ownership.
Hold for decades, not quarters. Most 100-baggers took 20+ years.
Focus on high returns on capital AND the ability to reinvest at those rates.
Ignore macro noise and short-term volatility. Use the coffee can approach.
Avoid companies that pay out earnings as dividends if they can reinvest profitably.
Bet big when odds are in your favor — the Kelly criterion argues for concentration.
Seek companies with wide moats that can fight mean reversion.
Simple, understandable businesses often produce the best long-term results.
Do not confuse a great company with a great stock — price matters.
whoShouldRead: Long-term value investors looking to compound wealth over decades
Anyone tired of short-term trading and seeking a patient, evidence-based approach
Investors building concentrated portfolios who want a framework for selection
Fans of Thomas Phelps, Peter Lynch, and Philip Fisher who want updated research
